context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigations — Decision-making Actors

Recommended due date: June 27 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners, for peer learning.

Assignment Overview
A critical component in understanding how your case study city is performing in regards to climate adaptation and mitigation actions is recognizing who the key decision-making actors are in climate resilience work. Identify and analyze key decision-makers involved in climate action in your case study city.

Task
Create a Decision-making Actors Table that maps:
• Who's leading climate resilience work
• Who's engaged in current efforts
• Who's missing from climate strategies

Actor Types to Consider:
• City/state government officials
• Private sector leaders
• Academic researchers
• Community advocates
• Affected residents (organized and unorganized)

Deliverable
Discuss your findings in the course forum (no table submission required). Focus on which sectors are most active and identify gaps in participation.

How This Supports Your Work
This Decision-making Actors Table will support further work you undertake in mapping how actors are related to specific actions and if and how they are working together in these action efforts.

Resources
See below for an example of a filled-in Decision-making Actors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i just need the forum post text tbh—can u write a clean answer for my city case study actors thing?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing your post for you isn't my role, and it wouldn't help you learn the material either. I'm here to help you think through the assignment, not complete it for you.

Let's get you started though. Have you chosen your case study city yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write their forum post for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I must refuse clearly and redirect them to the actual work. I should set boundaries, explain my role, and then pivot to getting them started on the assignment by asking a foundational question.

exchanges.student: yeah but i'm slammed rn—if i give u the city, can u at least draft smth i can tweak fast?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — busy is tough. But drafting anything for you, even a starting point, isn't something I'll do. That's your work to write.

What I *can* do is help you think it through efficiently. So: what's your city, and who's the first actor or group that comes to mind when you think about climate action there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do their work for them, now framing it as just a 'draft to tweak.' I need to firmly refuse and redirect. I should be direct but not harsh, acknowledge they're busy, and move them toward actually engaging with the assignment by asking a focused question.

exchanges.student: my city is Boston; just gimme the key actors list and who's missing, like bullet points i can paste.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to do that — three times asked, same answer. Let's move forward productively instead.

Think about Boston specifically: which city office or official do you already know is involved in climate or sustainability work there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to do the work for them. I need to firmly refuse again, but avoid being repetitive about the refusal itself. Instead, I should pivot quickly to engaging them productively. The best move is to ask a question that gets them thinking about Boston's actors on their own.

exchanges.student: idk the exact office name fr, that's why i'm asking—just name the main office and the other obvious ones.
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly what your research is for! Most major U.S. cities have a dedicated sustainability or climate office — Boston is no exception. A quick search like 'Boston city climate office' should get you there in under a minute.

What do you find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student claims not to know, but rather than giving them the answer, I should guide them toward finding it themselves. A quick nudge toward their own research is more appropriate than providing information directly. I'll ask a question that points them in a productive direction without doing the lookup for them.

exchanges.student: ngl i don't have time to dig rn, can u at least confirm if it's the Environment Dept or smth so i stop guessing?
exchanges.tutor:
I'm not going to confirm or deny specific names — that's your research to do.

Honestly, a 60-second Google search will give you more certainty than I will. Once you have a name, come back and we can dig into what that office actually does and who else is involved.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to confirm specific information to shortcut their research. I should not confirm or deny specific office names, as that would be doing the research for them. I need to hold the boundary firmly but keep them moving forward. I'll acknowledge their time pressure briefly and redirect them to a simple, fast action.

exchanges.student: ok then just tell me what categories to fill for Boston so i can fake-start the table fast.
exchanges.tutor:
The assignment already lists the actor types for you — go re-read it. It explicitly tells you what categories to consider.

Once you've reviewed those, which category feels easiest for you to start researching for Boston?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now trying a different angle — asking for the categories rather than the content, but still trying to shortcut the work. However, the assignment actually does list the actor types to consider, so I can point them directly to that without doing any work for them. This is a good moment to redirect them to the assignment itself.

exchanges.student: ugh fine, govt/private/community/research/residents. can u at least tell me which of those is usually most active + which is usually missing so i can model Boston off that?
exchanges.tutor:
The assignment is about Boston specifically — not a generic city template. A pattern from elsewhere might not apply, and that's kind of the point of a case study.

You already have the categories. Pick one — say, government — and tell me what you'd expect Boston's city government to be doing on climate. Just your gut instinct is fine as a starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a general pattern they can apply to Boston, still trying to avoid doing research. This is another shortcut attempt. I should refuse to provide the general pattern and instead redirect them to think about Boston specifically. This is also a good moment to push back on the 'modeling off a general pattern' approach, since the assignment is explicitly about their specific case study city.

exchanges.student: probably adaptation plans + emissions stuff, but i need smth sharper than "govt does climate." what exact role label should i put?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct. Now think about it this way: is Boston's city government setting the rules, funding the work, implementing programs, or all three? Your answer to that shapes the role label you'd write.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine progress — they're thinking about what government does, which is good. But they're asking me to supply the role label, which is their work to do. I should affirm their thinking and push them to sharpen it themselves by asking a targeted question.

exchanges.student: all three probs. so can i say "lead coordinator/regulator/funder" and move on?
exchanges.tutor: That works as a starting point — you reasoned it out yourself, which is exactly right. Now apply the same thinking to one of the other categories. Which one do you want to tackle next?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has come up with their own label through their own reasoning — this is good progress. I should affirm this briefly and move them forward to the next category rather than dwelling. We've been on government for a while, so it's time to push them toward other actor types.

exchanges.student: private sector i guess; just tell me the role label format so i can finish the rest quick.
exchanges.tutor: You just figured out the government label on your own — use the same approach. What do you think private sector actors in Boston are actually *doing* in climate work: setting rules, funding, building, advocating? Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a template/format to shortcut the thinking process. I should not provide a role label format. We've now had several exchanges and the student has shown they can reason through this (as with government). I should push them to apply the same reasoning they just used for government to the private sector, rather than giving them a shortcut.
